item.title: Body Sculpting
item.description:
I started out by applying a wash coat of shallac around the edges to stiffen up the wood fibers in preparation for the routing.

I used a 1/4 inch roundover bit over the top and back (but not going all the way to the ends of the horns). I was worried the curly maple might chip or tear out, but it was fine. A nice, clean, round cut was made.

I went back over it by hand to smooth it out. I am keeping the ends of the horns sharp, so the rounded edge fades into the tips.

After rounding over the back edges the same way, I used a disc sander to sculpt out a slight belly to the back surface where the guitar meets the body of the player. This was done partially to compensate for a low area in one of the cherry boards used for the neck-core. It was about 1/8 inch too shallow. So I scooped out the back a bit giving it a nice smooth contour. This does have a nice feel to it when held against the ribs in the playing position. You can see here that the back dips in slightly around the waist area.

I went over the back edges again, and hand faded the roundover into the sharp horn ends. The fade from round to sharp feels and looks just as I had hoped. Once the neck is carved some final adjustments to the body contour around the cutaways and horns will be made, but the major work to the body is now done.

Here is the back with another temporary coat of shellac.

Quite pleased!

item.title: Post Routing Repair 1
item.description:
This small triangle of the cherry wood broke out during routing. It was probably caused by an existing hairline crack in the end of the board.

I found a small matching piece of the cherry wood used for the body that fits into the corner pretty well. This is glued and secured tightly with tape overnight.

Then cut with a flush cut saw fairly close.

And then carefully chiseled down flush with the body.

Barely visible, except for one corner that chipped out. I will need to fill this with sawdust/glue putty. There are a few other very minor holes (bug or worm holes) in the cherry that have appeared which will also be filled prior to finishing.

item.title: Pickup Routing Part 2
item.description:
The pickup slots can now be routed to their final depth.

I am making the slots 9/16 inch deep to give plenty of room for the wires and mounting hardware below each pickup.

Pickup wire channels

Holes leading into the control cavity will also need to be made now. My plan is to use 3-wire JST SM connectors from the pickup lead wires to the circuit test harness, to make disconnection and reconnection easy during the circuit testing process. I was thinking of keeping the connectors also to be able to remove the pickups and/or control component assembly separately without having to de-solder the connections at later dates.

I am not sure if I will keep this permanently though, as these connectors might introduce a point of noise or failure in the future, and opt to directly solder all connections once the circuit design is finalized.

The male JST SM connectors can just fit through a 3/8 inch hole, so that is the size of wiring channel I am drilling out of the sides of the pickup slots.

The wire channel leads to the control cavity.

The same is done for the neck pickup.

Test Fit

A test fitting looks good.

The pickups will be straight and at their correct (and adjustable) heights once I get the mounting screws in place.

item.title: Fretboard Placement
item.description:
Now I will get the fretboard perfectly lined up and in locked into its final position in preparation for it to be glued down. Here is the method I have settled on.

Positioning

This is just a matter of lining up the center line of the fretboard with the guitar neck and body center line, and ensuring the zero-fret is at the predetermined measurement at the headstock end.

The fretboard is clamped firmly in place.

Dowel Indexing

The fretboard will be locked into position on the neck with two short dowels. This is crucial to prevent the fretboard from sliding out of position during gluing. I have used small brads into two frets before, but I have found they are unreliable and prone to movement from clamping forces and cause slippage. I now use 3/16 dowels instead, which are much more stable and keep the position locked in.

To get the dowel holes in the bottom of the fretboard and top face of the neck perfectly matched, I start by drilling 3/64 diameter holes using a hand crank drill. The holes are drilled in the fret slots of course so will not be visible once the frets are installed.

Here I am using the first and twentieth frets. These resulting holes are then drilled to about 3/32 deep using a 3/16 brad point bit. Now some sort sections of dowels can be used to lock the position.

The important thing here is to not drill into the truss rod channel or carbon fiber bar, so I need to come out about 9/16 from the center line.

I am using a hand powered drill and I made a little jig from acrylic to keep the bit straight up and down, being too lazy to go transfer this whole assembly to the drill press.

Now with two short 3/16 dowels in the underside of the fretboard, they lock into their respective holes in the neck. I can take it on and off multiple times and it always goes back into the exact spot, which is handy.

Preview

Here is a preview of things to come. Still a lot of excess wood to be removed from the neck, but I am keeping it there for now so it stays square and easy to hold in the vise.

item.title: Body Route
item.description:
Exciting times.

The guitar body can finally take shape.

First the outline is roughed out on the bandsaw. I left about 1/8 inch all around. If I had to do this again I would try to get closer, as even this is a lot of extra wood to remove by router.

Routing

I am using a 1/2 inch diameter by 1 inch deep top-bearing bit. This will require two passes to get the full 1.5 inch depth. This produced a ridiculous amount of dust and shavings.

Some shots of the first go round:

Now for the final pass.

Only two screw ups

Around the upper horn, the router jumped a little and caused a couple minor gouges in the side. I think/hope these will sand out.

The other boo boo is visible in the far right of this photo of the end. I do not think it was a routing error, but something went wrong with the glue up of the body sections and has caused a chunk to have broken out. I will have to fill this with an off-cut of the cherry section wood and try to smooth it out, but it will always be visible to some degree.

All other imperfections are no worry for now and will likely sand down nicely.

The Result

This is looking awesome if I do say so myself.

I would not do this again without using a table-mounted router.

item.title: Pickup Routing
item.description:
The pickups are here!

As planned, these are the quarter pound strat pickups, with optional tap wires. One is a reverse-wind, reverse-polarity (RWRP) so they will be hum-cancelling when used together in series.

The white Seymour Duncan logos are going to be removed. Sorry Seymour.

Now the slots can be routed into the guitar body. This is another example of why it is important to wait until the parts are on hand. These are measuring slightly larger than their online specs.

In search of the perfect 3/4 inch wide hotdog

The goal now is to create a routing template of the ideal proportion. I figure a slot of .75 high by 3.3875 wide will give about 1/32 gap around the edge. This will be made from the 1/4 inch thick acrylic. This took me three tries to get close enough to what I consider acceptable. I started by boring the two end holes with a 3/4 inch forstener bit. Then the challenge is to connect the holes precisely. On the third try, I used the router indexed to the edge of the acrylic sheet to cut the slot edges.

Now for a test route on some scrap.

The fit seems good!

Placement

The locations of the pickup slots are plotted onto the guitar, taking care to ensure they are centered and at the proper angle. The angle is purely for visual consistency with the two string scale lengths. The bridge pickup is 14 degrees, and the neck is 11.21 degrees. These angles are based on the how the frets would lay if extended down this far.

Routing

At this stage, I am not going to route the slots to their final depth. The goal now is to route them just deep enough so that the final depth can be routed later without the template, and the router bit bearing can roll on the existing sides. The reason for this is that once the guitar is cut out there will be no good place to attach the template. But I also want to screw down the template into the waste wood of the pickup slots. So I need to preserve some waste wood in the these slot areas for now. Had to do some thinking to plan this out.

First some starter holes.

Now to route the slots.

Done for now. They are just deep enough that the router bearing can use the existing route as its own template for remainder of the operation.

item.title: Super K Top
item.description:
I have three Englemann Spruce top sets to pick from. I got these from StewMac over the course of a few years, who sells them as tops for small acoustic guitars and are not very expensive. This is the first time I am using Engelmann Spruce (Picea engelmannii) for an instrument sound board, although I have had good luck using it as bracing in ukuleles. I used Sitka spruce on the first Super K.

The Engelmann is lighter and softer than Sitka and allegedly more complex and nuanced sound-wise, suitable for a light touch as opposed to aggressive strumming. I think it may be a good match for the type of tone and style these guitars lend themselves to - chord-melody, jazzy lead lines, and laid-back comping. This should result in an interesting comparison.

Selection

Picking the wood for the top. I am not quite sure how to go about it - they all have a nice tap tone, altough each one has its own character. I am probably mostly going by visual appearance.

Top 1 looks ok, is fairly clear, although has some barely visible dark spots.

Top 2 is has some interesting grain lines.

Top 3 looks to be from the same log as top 2. It has some weird dark streaks close to the center.

For some reason, I seem to like top number 2. The grain lines seems to be almost guitar shaped!

Jointing

Using the vise-mounted plane jig to get the edges jointed. The idea is to get the seam where no light comes when pressed together edge to edge.

Glue Up

This is my preferred method for joining tops. String and wedges. It is supposedly an old Spanish guitar building technique. (My wedges need to be bigger next time.)

Cut out

The glue line is scraped down and both surfaces sanded to 220. The final location of the outline is determined, using the mould as a guide. I also draw a light pencil line down the center.

The top is cut out on the bandsaw, about 1/16 - 1/8 inch out from the line. The guitar will have binding so cutting close is not a problem. Some useful scraps remain and are saved.

The edges and top surface get a wash coat of shellac to protect the delicate spruce during the rest of the build. This will all be sanded off before finishing. The inside gets no shellac (yet) since it will be a gluing surface.

The shellac also seals the end grain to deter cracks while drying.

item.title: Body Assembly
item.description:
First to plug the temporary air holes leading into the hollow chambers.

Some 3/8 plugs of flat sawn walnut are glued in.

Chiseled flush.

Jointing the edges

Now for the task of getting the joints to mate perfectly. This is done almost entirely with the #7 plane. I use a .0025 feeler gauge to check the seam for any gaps.

At one point I tried to fix some high and low spots with a card scraper. This never worked perfectly. Best to trust the plane. When the plane finally takes off a long shaving of the full length of the joint, it is right. Only one or two places and the ends where the thin metal feeler gauge gets through. At some point you have to stop and say it is as good as it will get.

This step was probably the most challenging part of the entire build so far.

Dowels to aid in glueing

I use some short, no longer than 3/8 of an inch, 1/4 inch oak dowels to keep the joints in place while gluing. These are only to keep the pieces from sliding around during the glue up. Not for any structural reinforcement. They are kept very short and the holes as shallow as possible to prevent future stress on the wood.

Both sides just about ready!

Blue side

I glued the blue side first. I was able to use my 8 inch C clamps here, which apply a great deal of pressure and ensure any imperfections in the seam are pressed together flat.

This side joint had some gap issues near the lower end which press together almost completely with the strong C clamps. I stuck some maple plane shavings into the glue of any remaining gap, which should blend right in with the wood invisibly.

The operation was a success!

Red side

Now for the red side. Too wide for any C clamps, so I had to use three bar clamps. Pipe clamps would have been better. Fortunately, this side joint mated very closely and did not required excessive pressure. Although I did tighten the bar clamps as much as possible, to the point the the bars really flexed.

The Result

Overall I am happy with how this turned out. It is starting to look like something.

Some work with the plane again to get the top flat. Curly tiger maple is looking fantastic.

Major milestone reached!

Next up: routing.

Only one more glue joint to make on the entire guitar.

item.title: Fretboard Part 6
item.description:
Now to prepare the fretboard and binding for the glue up.

To being with, I create a layer consisting of masking tape, double sided tape, and craft paper on the bottom of the fretboard. This is so that the binding strips extend down past the bottom edge when being glued down, creating some excess binding on the bottom. This is to try to avoid the binding not coming all the way down to the bottom edge of the fretboard which would be disastrous.

To keep glue out of the slots I wedge some folded Tyvek into each slot, as well as tape off the nut slot.

Each strip is given a final sanding down to 1000 grit and cleaned of any dust.

First the bottom end binding is glued on, made from an off-cut of one of the strips.

Each side piece is glued on, one at a time. The jig is simply a couple strips of particle board screwed together to form a corner with some cork added to the inside edge.

Note use of binding tape to help pull tight, and auxiliary hold-down clamps to keep fretboard flat.

Now all three edges have binding. What looks rough now will soon clean up.

Here is a shot of the bottom showing the excess binding coming proud of the edge as I intended, thanks to the backer layer.

The excess is planed off the top and bottom using a micro plane, and sanded smooth.

Finally, the edges are sanded smooth and square again, taking off the extra 1/32 of an inch and making the bindings their target 1/16 inch thickness. The fretboard is now at the target dimensions, with the exception of the radius across the top. There is also a bit of extra length at the headstock end which I am leaving on for now until final placement on the neck is determined.

Done with the fretboard until final assembly!

item.title: Fretboard Part 5
item.description:
I am going to try adding the side dots to the player-side (blue side) fretboard edge binding before attaching the binding.

For the dots, I am using the curly maple wood used on the rest of the guitar.

Here is the process I have come up with for making wood side dots.

First I cut out ten small blocks and glue them to the ends of dowels with CA gel glue. The grain direction is important and I want the quartersawn side facing out, not the end grain. (Otherwise I would have just used lengthwise sticks.)

I mark the grain direction up side, and roughly sand the blocks to match the cylindrical shape of the dowel.

These are then sharpened to conical points using a pencil sharpener.

Now the 10 points are finely sanded and ready for installation.

The 3/32 diameter holes for the dots are then drilled into a binding strip at the precise locations of the intended fret marker locations.

The vertical position of the line of dots is slightly below center to take into account the resulting fretboard thickness at the edges after being sanded to a 12 inch radius.

Now the dot points are glued into the holes. I do this in two sessions since the 12th fret double-dot markers are too close to be done at the same time. (This explains why I made five double-ended dowels instead of ten.)

The points are then cut off flush and sanded, making some nice looking dots.

Now this binding strip and the matching strip for the other side are ready to be glued to the fretboard.
